titleInfo.title: Monks and Monarchs, Kinship and Kingship: Tanqian in Sui Buddhism and Politics

mods.abstract: About Tanqian 曇遷 (542-607), "the primary architect behind the project of turning Buddhism into the supreme state ideology of the Sui" (p. 9). He was abbot of the Chandingsi founded in 603, where 120 meditation masters lived. Chapter four, "Six meditation groups in the 6th and 7th centuries", 149-179: An analysis of Daoxuan's Xichan lun 習禪論 (T#2060, 50:595c26-597b23). There were at least six meditation traditions in China from the beginning of the sixth to the middle of the seventh century. Sengchou's tradition was most influential, while Bodhidharma's tradition is considered unorthodox. All groups with the exception of Bodhidharma's were compatible with each other. Chapter five, "Tanqian and the twin Chandingsi monasteries": Chandingsi founded in 603 to honor Empress Wenxian 文獻(the imperial consort Empress Dugu 獨孤皇后, 553-602). It was the headquarter of a national system of meditation centers, meant to regenerate the meditation tradition of Sengchou. Da Chandingsi founded in 605 under Sui Yangdi, commemorating his father.

name.namePart: Scuola Italiana di Studi sull’Asia Orientale
Italian School of East Asian Studies

titleInfo.title: Italian School of East Asian Studies, Essays
